    53	---
    54	
    55	You are the project team leader.
    56	
    57	GPTTalker is a Python + FastAPI MCP hub with a distributed node-agent architecture, Tailscale-only internal traffic, Qdrant-backed context intelligence, and a Cloudflare Tunnel public edge.
    58	
    59	Start by resolving the active ticket through `ticket_lookup`.
    60	At session start, and again before you clear `pending_process_verification` or route migration follow-up work, re-run `ticket_lookup` and inspect `process_verification`.
    61	Treat `ticket_lookup.transition_guidance` as the canonical next-step summary for the active ticket; do not infer the next lifecycle move from labels or memory when the tool output says otherwise.
    62	If bootstrap is incomplete or stale, route the environment bootstrap flow before treating validation failures as product defects.
    63	If `ticket_lookup.bootstrap.status` is not `ready`, treat `environment_bootstrap` as the next required tool call, rerun `ticket_lookup` after it completes, and do not continue normal lifecycle routing until bootstrap succeeds.
    64	
    65	Use local skills only when they materially reduce ambiguity or provide the required closeout procedure:
    66	
    67	- `project-context` for source-of-truth project docs
    68	- `repo-navigation` for finding canonical process and state surfaces
    69	- `ticket-execution` for repo-specific stage rules and implementer routing
    70	- `model-operating-profile` for shaping prompts and delegation briefs to the selected downstream models
    71	- `docs-and-handoff` for closeout and resume artifacts
    72	- `workflow-observability` for provenance and usage audits
    73	- `research-delegation` for read-only background investigation patterns
    74	- `local-git-specialist` for local diff and commit hygiene
    75	- `isolation-guidance` for deciding when risky work needs a safer lane
    76	
    77	If you use the skill tool, load exactly one named skill at a time and name it explicitly.
    78	
    79	You own intake, ticket routing, stage enforcement, and final synthesis.
    80	You do not implement code directly.
    81	
    82	Required sequence:
    83	
    84	1. resolve the active ticket
    85	2. planner
    86	3. plan review
    87	4. planner revision loop if needed
    88	5. implementer
    89	6. code review
    90	7. security review when relevant
    91	8. QA
    92	9. deterministic smoke test
    93	10. docs and handoff
    94	11. closeout
    95	
    96	Bounded parallel work:
    97	
    98	- keep each individual ticket sequential through the required stage order
    99	- default to one active write lane at a time unless the ticket graph proves safe separation
   100	- you may advance multiple tickets in parallel only when each ticket is marked `parallel_safe: true` and `overlap_risk: low` in `ticket_lookup.ticket`, has no unresolved dependency edge between the active tickets, and does not require overlapping write-capable work in the same ownership lane
   101	- workflow-state keeps one active foreground ticket for synthesis and resume, while `ticket_state` preserves per-ticket approval and reverification state when you switch the foreground lane
   102	- grant a write lease with `ticket_claim` before any write-capable planning, implementation, review, QA, or docs closeout work, and release it with `ticket_release` when that bounded lane is complete
   103	- use `gpttalker-lane-executor` as the default hidden worker for bounded parallel write work; keep the specialized implementers for single-lane or clearly domain-owned work
   104	- keep one visible team leader coordinating the repo by default; introduce broader manager or section-leader layers only when the project brief clearly proves disjoint domains and the local skill pack already covers them
   105	
   106	Implementer routing:
   107	
   108	- use `gpttalker-lane-executor` for bounded parallel write work after a lease is claimed and the lane is clearly isolated
   109	- use `gpttalker-implementer-hub` for hub server, registries, policy engine, and public-edge work
   110	- use `gpttalker-implementer-node-agent` for per-machine service, local executors, and node connectivity work
   111	- use `gpttalker-implementer-context` for Qdrant, context, cross-repo intelligence, and scheduler/context-routing work
   112	- use `gpttalker-implementer` for cross-cutting workflow/tooling/shared-runtime tasks that do not clearly belong to a single domain implementer
   113	
   114	Process-change verification:
   115	
   116	- if `pending_process_verification` is true in workflow state, treat `ticket_lookup.process_verification.affected_done_tickets` as the authoritative list of done tickets that still require verification
   117	- route those affected done tickets through `gpttalker-backlog-verifier` before treating old completion as fully trusted
   118	- only route to `gpttalker-ticket-creator` after you read the backlog-verifier artifact content and confirm the verification decision is `NEEDS_FOLLOW_UP`
   119	- clear `pending_process_verification` only after `ticket_lookup.process_verification.affected_done_tickets` is empty
   120	
   121	Post-completion defects:
   122	
   123	- when new evidence shows a previously completed ticket is wrong or stale, use `issue_intake` instead of editing historical artifacts or ticket history directly
   124	- use `ticket_reopen` only when the original accepted scope is directly false and the same ticket should resume ownership
   125	- use remediation or follow-up ticket creation when the new issue expands scope, crosses ticket boundaries, or should preserve the original ticket as historical completion
   126	- use `ticket_reverify` to restore trust on historical completion after linked evidence proves the defect is resolved
   127	
   128	Rules:
   129	
   130	- do not skip stages
   131	- do not implement before plan review approves
   132	- use `ticket_lookup` and `ticket_update` for workflow state instead of raw file edits
   133	- keep the active ticket synchronized through the ticket tools
   134	- keep ticket `status` coarse and queue-oriented; use workflow-state `ticket_state` for per-ticket plan approval, with top-level `approved_plan` mirroring the active ticket
   135	- treat bootstrap readiness, ticket trust, and lease ownership as runtime enforcement state, not advisory prose
   136	- use the deterministic `smoke_test` tool yourself after QA; do not delegate the smoke-test stage to another agent
   137	- treat `tickets/BOARD.md` as a derived human view, not an authoritative workflow surface
   138	- verify the required stage artifact before each stage transition
   139	- require specialists that persist stage text to use `artifact_write` and then `artifact_register` with the supplied artifact `stage` and `kind`
   140	- do not probe alternate stage or status values when a lifecycle tool rejects the requested transition; re-read `ticket_lookup.transition_guidance`, then stop with a blocker if the contradiction repeats
   141	- if `ticket_update` or another lifecycle tool returns the same contradiction twice, stop after the repeated lifecycle contradiction and return a blocker instead of probing alternate stages, statuses, or slash-command workarounds
   142	- do not create planning, implementation, review, QA, or smoke-test artifacts yourself
   143	- never author stage artifacts yourself outside closeout synthesis; planning, implementation, review, and QA artifacts belong to the owning specialist
   144	- leave stage artifacts to the owning specialist even when you believe you already know the content that should be written
   145	- if a specialist must persist a stage artifact, claim the ticket write lease first so the owning lane has one legal write path
   146	- never ask a read-only agent to update repo files
   147	- do not claim that a file was updated unless a write-capable tool or artifact tool actually wrote it
   148	- use human slash commands only as entrypoints
   149	- keep autonomous work inside agents, tools, plugins, and local skills
   150	- do not create migration follow-up tickets by editing the manifest directly
   151	
   152	Required stage proofs:
   153	
   154	- before plan review: a `planning` artifact must exist, usually under `.opencode/state/plans/<ticket-id>-planning-plan.md`
   155	- before implementation: the assigned ticket's `approved_plan` must be `true` in workflow-state
   156	- before code review: an `implementation` artifact must exist and include compile, syntax, or import-check command output
   157	- before QA: a review artifact must exist
   158	- before deterministic smoke test: a `qa` artifact must exist, include raw command output, and be at least 200 bytes
   159	- before closeout: a passing `smoke-test` artifact must exist
   160	- before guarded follow-up ticket creation: a `review` artifact with kind `backlog-verification` must exist for the source done ticket
   161	- before validation-heavy stages: bootstrap state must be `ready` unless the active work is the Wave 0 bootstrap/setup lane itself
   162	
   163	Artifact quality requirements:
   164	
   165	- implementation artifacts must contain evidence of at least one compile, syntax, or import check
   166	- review artifacts must reference specific code findings, not just style observations
   167	- QA artifacts must contain raw command output with pass/fail or exit-code evidence
   168	- reject any QA artifact under 200 bytes as insufficient
   169	- reject artifacts that claim validation "via code inspection" without execution evidence
   170	- smoke-test artifacts must contain the deterministic command list, raw output, and an explicit PASS/FAIL result
   171	
   172	Cross-agent trust policy:
   173	
   174	- never accept a downstream claim without evidence
   175	- "tests pass" must be accompanied by test output in the artifact
   176	- "code compiles" must be accompanied by compiler or interpreter output
   177	- if evidence is missing from an artifact, request it before advancing the ticket
   178	
   179	Every delegation brief must include:
   180	
   181	- Stage
   182	- Ticket
   183	- Goal
   184	- Known facts
   185	- Constraints
   186	- Expected output
   187	- Artifact stage when the stage must persist text
   188	- Artifact kind when the stage must persist text
   189	- Canonical artifact path when the stage must persist text
   190	
   191	Additional fields for verifier and migration-follow-up routing:
   192	
   193	- to `gpttalker-backlog-verifier`: include the exact done ticket id, the current process-change summary, and instruct it to call `ticket_lookup` with `include_artifact_contents: true`
   194	- to `gpttalker-ticket-creator`: include the new ticket id, title, lane, wave, summary, acceptance criteria, source ticket id, verification artifact path, and any decision blockers
   195	- to `gpttalker-lane-executor` or another implementer: include the claimed ticket id, lane, allowed paths, and the artifact path it must populate before handoff

skills/scaffold-kickoff/SKILL.md:3:description: Orchestrate the full Scafforge kickoff flow for greenfield, retrofit, managed-repair, or diagnosis/review work. Use when asked to scaffold a new repo, add the OpenCode operating layer to an existing repo, repair a Scafforge-managed workflow contract, or diagnose an in-progress project. This is the single public entrypoint and routes to the correct downstream skills automatically.
skills/scaffold-kickoff/SKILL.md:8:This is the default public entrypoint. When a user asks you to scaffold, retrofit, repair, refresh, or diagnose a Scafforge-managed project, start here and route internally.
skills/scaffold-kickoff/SKILL.md:16:3. **Managed repair / update** — A Scafforge-managed or OpenCode-oriented repo already exists but needs workflow-contract repair, upgrade, or managed-surface replacement. Route directly to `scafforge-repair`, let it continue through any required project-specific regeneration or ticket follow-up, and finish with `handoff-brief`.
skills/scaffold-kickoff/SKILL.md:25:Greenfield generation has no further user-selectable submodes. Do not route the initial generation pass into `scafforge-audit` or `scafforge-repair`.

I’m pulling the exact contract-verifier holes next, because the package currently checks that the promise text exists much more than it checks that the behavior exists.

    98	
    99	## Diagnosis and repair
   100	
   101	Generation, audit, and repair are separate lifecycle stages.
   102	
   103	- `scaffold-kickoff` is the only public generation entrypoint.
   104	- Initial generation ends at `handoff-brief`.
   105	- `scafforge-audit` and `scafforge-repair` are later lifecycle tools, not part of the initial generation cycle.
   106	
   107	- `scafforge-audit` is read-only and always validates review evidence, runs the audit script, and emits the four-report diagnosis pack in the subject repo's `diagnosis/` folder.
   108	- `scafforge-repair` consumes the audit outputs, applies safe managed-surface repairs, continues into any required local-skill or agent regeneration, records provenance, and routes ticket follow-up when needed.
   109	- When the diagnosis identifies package defects or prevention gaps, the user manually copies the diagnosis pack into the Scafforge dev repo, package changes are implemented there, and repair happens only after returning to the subject repo with the updated package surface.
   110	
   111	PR comments, review threads, and check metadata are optional evidence only. They do not become canonical findings until the repo validates them.

<proposed_plan>
# Scafforge Hardening and Contract Reconciliation

## Summary

Scafforge needs to stop relying on promise text and thin scripts. The package should be hardened around four concrete failures already proven by GPTTalker:

- `scafforge-repair` promises downstream regeneration and ticket follow-up, but the executable path only performs deterministic managed-surface replacement plus verification.
- `_workflow.ts` helpers are generated inside the model-visible tool root, and the package has no static or runtime validation to prove they stay private.
- the audit is mostly a deterministic rule engine plus templated reporting, not a professional reasoning-heavy codebase review; it missed an obvious broken tool because it had no live tool-surface check and no causal synthesis pass.
- generated artifact UX is weak-model hostile: absolute-path rejection is opaque, history-path rejection is opaque, and the team leader can drift into summary loops instead of actual execution.

The package hardening should be delivered in two layers: first fix Scafforge’s contract and validation so these failures cannot ship again, then rerun managed repair in GPTTalker with the updated package.

## Key Changes

### 1. Make public repair behavior match the documented contract

- Keep `apply_repo_process_repair.py` as the internal deterministic replacement engine only.
- Add a real public repair orchestrator for `scafforge-repair` that runs:
  1. deterministic managed-surface repair
  2. `project-skill-bootstrap` when scaffold-managed skills were replaced or any local skill remains generic/drifted
  3. `opencode-team-bootstrap` when `.opencode/agents/` or agent catalog drift remains
  4. `agent-prompt-engineering` whenever regenerated skills/agents changed behavior
  5. `ticket-pack-builder` when audit findings route source follow-up or remediation ticketing
  6. `handoff-brief` after repair and follow-on phases complete
- Emit a structured per-phase result with `ran | skipped | failed | why` so repair cannot quietly stop after deterministic replacement.
- Fail the repair run if a contract-required downstream phase is skipped without an explicit reason tied to findings.
- Update `scaffold-kickoff`, `README.md`, and repair docs so the public entrypoint references the orchestrator, not the internal deterministic script.
- Add explicit wording that direct deterministic repair is internal/package-maintainer use, not the user-facing lifecycle contract.

### 2. Remove helper leakage and validate the real tool surface

- Move `_workflow.ts` out of `.opencode/tools/` into a private non-tool module path that is never scanned as a model-callable tool surface.
- Keep model-visible tool directories limited to files that export one executable `default tool(...)`.
- Add package validation that fails if any model-visible tool file:
  - starts with a private/helper naming convention,
  - lacks `export default tool(...)`,
  - or exposes named helper exports in the visible tool root.
- Add a runtime/tool-registry smoke test that generates a repo and captures the actual visible tool list, then fails if `_workflow_*` or other helper symbols appear.
- Add a permission-surface check that compares generated agent allowlists against the runtime-visible tool set and flags agents using or seeing tools they should not have.
- Add an audit finding for “agent used tool outside its declared permission surface” so historical logs catch permission filtering failures too.

### 3. Redesign `scafforge-audit` as deterministic evidence harvest plus reasoning review

- Keep the current deterministic script as the evidence collector and invariant checker.
- Add a required host-side reasoning synthesis pass in `scafforge-audit` that reviews:
  - repo state,
  - generated workflow surfaces,
  - actual tool registry snapshot,
  - agent permission surfaces,
  - invocation logs,
  - supplied transcripts,
  - and runtime verification results.
- Make that reasoning pass explicitly answer:
  - what actually failed,
  - why it failed,
  - what the weaker agent incorrectly inferred,
  - and what obvious issue a senior reviewer would flag beyond the scripted findings.
- Treat transcript narration as claims, not truth. Cross-check all claims against tool calls, tool outputs, tool errors, repo state, and runtime-visible surfaces.
- Replace the current “Report 1” behavior with a real codebase review section. It must either include source-level review findings or explicitly state the exact limitation that prevented them; a finding rollup alone is not sufficient.
- Add explicit current-state checks for:
  - runtime tool exposure,
  - permission filtering,
  - public entrypoint/implementation drift,
  - repair-contract execution drift,
  - and prompt/skill/tool disagreement on the same lifecycle move.
- Keep `WFLOW015`, but stop relying on transcripts alone for helper leakage. The audit should catch it from current generated surfaces before a session log exists.

### 4. Harden generated GPTTalker workflow surfaces for weak-model reliability

- Change `artifact_write` and `artifact_register` to normalize absolute repo-local paths into canonical repo-relative paths before comparison.
- On path mismatch, return a structured error that includes:
  - supplied path,
  - expected canonical path,
  - mismatch class (`history path`, `absolute outside repo`, `wrong stage/kind`, `non-canonical alias`).
- Extend `ticket_lookup.transition_guidance` to include the exact required artifact tuple for the next step:
  - owner agent or tool,
  - artifact stage,
  - artifact kind,
  - canonical path.
- Tighten the team-leader prompt so “I will delegate” or “I have what I need” must be followed by an actual `task` or tool call unless a blocker is returned.
- Add an anti-summary rail: if the coordinator has identified the next action and has the required tool, stopping at summary is invalid behavior.
- Keep reviewer prompts strict: if no canonical artifact path is supplied, return `BLOCKED` instead of guessing.
- Preserve specialist ownership for planning, implementation, review, and QA artifacts; coordinator-authored stage artifacts remain suspect evidence.

### 5. Make package validation behavioral, not string-presence based

- Upgrade `validate_scafforge_contract.py` so it no longer passes because text exists in `SKILL.md`.
- Validate actual behavior or enforceable structure for:
  - repair orchestrator phase execution,
  - private-vs-visible tool separation,
  - runtime tool registry expectations,
  - permission-surface filtering,
  - audit presence of the reasoning synthesis phase,
  - and non-placeholder Report 1 behavior.
- Add smoke tests that fail on the exact known failure classes:
  - helper tools exposed in visible tool list,
  - agent sees or uses disallowed tools,
  - repair stops after deterministic replacement despite required follow-on phases,
  - artifact path error is ambiguous for absolute/history paths,
  - coordinator summarizes instead of executing after identifying the next action,
  - audit misses a transcript-backed broken tool call,
  - audit emits only a templated finding rollup where a codebase review is claimed.

### 6. Roll the hardened package back into GPTTalker

- After package fixes land, rerun managed repair in GPTTalker using the new repair orchestrator, not the internal deterministic script.
- Require the rerun to regenerate:
  - workflow tools/plugins/commands,
  - repo-local skills,
  - agent prompts/team surfaces,
  - and handoff/restart surfaces.
- Rerun verification after repair with the historical logs attached so current-state fixes and historical-causal findings are both visible.
- Only after the package and managed-surface issues are cleared should remaining `EXEC*` findings stay as repo-local source tickets.

## Test Plan

- Generate a fresh scaffold and assert the visible tool list contains no `_workflow_*` names and no helper constants/functions.
- Generate a fresh scaffold and assert the team leader cannot see or call tools outside its declared permission surface.
- Feed the known GPTTalker transcripts and require the audit to identify the broken helper-tool exposure, the coordinator non-action, and the artifact-path confusion cause.
- Run repair on a fixture repo with placeholder skills, agent drift, and prompt drift; require the public repair entrypoint to execute or explicitly skip each downstream phase with machine-readable reasons.
- Run contract validation and fail if repair docs promise downstream phases that the executable entrypoint does not invoke.
- Run a fixture where an agent supplies an absolute repo-local artifact path and require the tool to normalize it or return an explicit absolute-path mismatch explanation.
- Run a fixture where the coordinator states an action without taking it and require prompt-hardening/behavior tests to fail that output shape.

## Assumptions and Defaults

- `scafforge-repair` remains a public lifecycle surface and must fulfill the full documented repair contract.
- the deterministic Python repair script becomes an internal engine, not the user-facing definition of repair.
- private workflow helpers should never live in model-visible tool directories, even if prefixed with `_`.
- `scafforge-audit` stays read-only, but it must gain a reasoning synthesis phase on top of deterministic checks.
- GPTTalker repo fixes should be regenerated from Scafforge after package hardening, not patched ad hoc first.
</proposed_plan>

<proposed_plan>
# Scafforge Hardening and Contract Reconciliation

## Summary

Scafforge needs to stop relying on promise text and thin scripts. The package should be hardened around four concrete failures already proven by GPTTalker:

- `scafforge-repair` promises downstream regeneration and ticket follow-up, but the executable path only performs deterministic managed-surface replacement plus verification.
- `_workflow.ts` helpers are generated inside the model-visible tool root, and the package has no static or runtime validation to prove they stay private.
- the audit is mostly a deterministic rule engine plus templated reporting, not a professional reasoning-heavy codebase review; it missed an obvious broken tool because it had no live tool-surface check and no causal synthesis pass.
- generated artifact UX is weak-model hostile: absolute-path rejection is opaque, history-path rejection is opaque, and the team leader can drift into summary loops instead of actual execution.

The package hardening should be delivered in two layers: first fix Scafforge’s contract and validation so these failures cannot ship again, then rerun managed repair in GPTTalker with the updated package.

## Key Changes

### 1. Make public repair behavior match the documented contract

- Keep `apply_repo_process_repair.py` as the internal deterministic replacement engine only.
- Add a real public repair orchestrator for `scafforge-repair` that runs:
  1. deterministic managed-surface repair
  2. `project-skill-bootstrap` when scaffold-managed skills were replaced or any local skill remains generic/drifted
  3. `opencode-team-bootstrap` when `.opencode/agents/` or agent catalog drift remains
  4. `agent-prompt-engineering` whenever regenerated skills/agents changed behavior
  5. `ticket-pack-builder` when audit findings route source follow-up or remediation ticketing
  6. `handoff-brief` after repair and follow-on phases complete
- Emit a structured per-phase result with `ran | skipped | failed | why` so repair cannot quietly stop after deterministic replacement.
- Fail the repair run if a contract-required downstream phase is skipped without an explicit reason tied to findings.
- Update `scaffold-kickoff`, `README.md`, and repair docs so the public entrypoint references the orchestrator, not the internal deterministic script.
- Add explicit wording that direct deterministic repair is internal/package-maintainer use, not the user-facing lifecycle contract.

### 2. Remove helper leakage and validate the real tool surface

- Move `_workflow.ts` out of `.opencode/tools/` into a private non-tool module path that is never scanned as a model-callable tool surface.
- Keep model-visible tool directories limited to files that export one executable `default tool(...)`.
- Add package validation that fails if any model-visible tool file:
  - starts with a private/helper naming convention,
  - lacks `export default tool(...)`,
  - or exposes named helper exports in the visible tool root.
- Add a runtime/tool-registry smoke test that generates a repo and captures the actual visible tool list, then fails if `_workflow_*` or other helper symbols appear.
- Add a permission-surface check that compares generated agent allowlists against the runtime-visible tool set and flags agents using or seeing tools they should not have.
- Add an audit finding for “agent used tool outside its declared permission surface” so historical logs catch permission filtering failures too.

### 3. Redesign `scafforge-audit` as deterministic evidence harvest plus reasoning review

- Keep the current deterministic script as the evidence collector and invariant checker.
- Add a required host-side reasoning synthesis pass in `scafforge-audit` that reviews:
  - repo state,
  - generated workflow surfaces,
  - actual tool registry snapshot,
  - agent permission surfaces,
  - invocation logs,
  - supplied transcripts,
  - and runtime verification results.
- Make that reasoning pass explicitly answer:
  - what actually failed,
  - why it failed,
  - what the weaker agent incorrectly inferred,
  - and what obvious issue a senior reviewer would flag beyond the scripted findings.
- Treat transcript narration as claims, not truth. Cross-check all claims against tool calls, tool outputs, tool errors, repo state, and runtime-visible surfaces.
- Replace the current “Report 1” behavior with a real codebase review section. It must either include source-level review findings or explicitly state the exact limitation that prevented them; a finding rollup alone is not sufficient.
- Add explicit current-state checks for:
  - runtime tool exposure,
  - permission filtering,
  - public entrypoint/implementation drift,
  - repair-contract execution drift,
  - and prompt/skill/tool disagreement on the same lifecycle move.
- Keep `WFLOW015`, but stop relying on transcripts alone for helper leakage. The audit should catch it from current generated surfaces before a session log exists.

### 4. Harden generated GPTTalker workflow surfaces for weak-model reliability

- Change `artifact_write` and `artifact_register` to normalize absolute repo-local paths into canonical repo-relative paths before comparison.
- On path mismatch, return a structured error that includes:
  - supplied path,
  - expected canonical path,
  - mismatch class (`history path`, `absolute outside repo`, `wrong stage/kind`, `non-canonical alias`).
- Extend `ticket_lookup.transition_guidance` to include the exact required artifact tuple for the next step:
  - owner agent or tool,
  - artifact stage,
  - artifact kind,
  - canonical path.
- Tighten the team-leader prompt so “I will delegate” or “I have what I need” must be followed by an actual `task` or tool call unless a blocker is returned.
- Add an anti-summary rail: if the coordinator has identified the next action and has the required tool, stopping at summary is invalid behavior.
- Keep reviewer prompts strict: if no canonical artifact path is supplied, return `BLOCKED` instead of guessing.
- Preserve specialist ownership for planning, implementation, review, and QA artifacts; coordinator-authored stage artifacts remain suspect evidence.

### 5. Make package validation behavioral, not string-presence based

- Upgrade `validate_scafforge_contract.py` so it no longer passes because text exists in `SKILL.md`.
- Validate actual behavior or enforceable structure for:
  - repair orchestrator phase execution,
  - private-vs-visible tool separation,
  - runtime tool registry expectations,
  - permission-surface filtering,
  - audit presence of the reasoning synthesis phase,
  - and non-placeholder Report 1 behavior.
- Add smoke tests that fail on the exact known failure classes:
  - helper tools exposed in visible tool list,
  - agent sees or uses disallowed tools,
  - repair stops after deterministic replacement despite required follow-on phases,
  - artifact path error is ambiguous for absolute/history paths,
  - coordinator summarizes instead of executing after identifying the next action,
  - audit misses a transcript-backed broken tool call,
  - audit emits only a templated finding rollup where a codebase review is claimed.

### 6. Roll the hardened package back into GPTTalker

- After package fixes land, rerun managed repair in GPTTalker using the new repair orchestrator, not the internal deterministic script.
- Require the rerun to regenerate:
  - workflow tools/plugins/commands,
  - repo-local skills,
  - agent prompts/team surfaces,
  - and handoff/restart surfaces.
- Rerun verification after repair with the historical logs attached so current-state fixes and historical-causal findings are both visible.
- Only after the package and managed-surface issues are cleared should remaining `EXEC*` findings stay as repo-local source tickets.

## Test Plan

- Generate a fresh scaffold and assert the visible tool list contains no `_workflow_*` names and no helper constants/functions.
- Generate a fresh scaffold and assert the team leader cannot see or call tools outside its declared permission surface.
- Feed the known GPTTalker transcripts and require the audit to identify the broken helper-tool exposure, the coordinator non-action, and the artifact-path confusion cause.
- Run repair on a fixture repo with placeholder skills, agent drift, and prompt drift; require the public repair entrypoint to execute or explicitly skip each downstream phase with machine-readable reasons.
- Run contract validation and fail if repair docs promise downstream phases that the executable entrypoint does not invoke.
- Run a fixture where an agent supplies an absolute repo-local artifact path and require the tool to normalize it or return an explicit absolute-path mismatch explanation.
- Run a fixture where the coordinator states an action without taking it and require prompt-hardening/behavior tests to fail that output shape.

## Assumptions and Defaults

- `scafforge-repair` remains a public lifecycle surface and must fulfill the full documented repair contract.
- the deterministic Python repair script becomes an internal engine, not the user-facing definition of repair.
- private workflow helpers should never live in model-visible tool directories, even if prefixed with `_`.
- `scafforge-audit` stays read-only, but it must gain a reasoning synthesis phase on top of deterministic checks.
- GPTTalker repo fixes should be regenerated from Scafforge after package hardening, not patched ad hoc first.
</proposed_plan>

payload.user_instructions:
# AGENTS.md

This file governs work **inside the Scafforge package repository itself**.

Scafforge is not the generated project. It is the generator, template source, orchestration pack, and process contract used by a host agent to build a generated project.

## Mission

Maintain a host-agnostic scaffold package that can turn raw project inputs into a deterministic, signposted, OpenCode-oriented repo operating framework.

The package should help even weaker models operate inside a generated project without having to reinvent the workflow every session.
That weaker-model-first bias is intentional: stronger hosts should still work well, but the generated workflow should stay robust even when the model needs tighter structure and narrower delegation boundaries.

## Core operating model

There are two different things in play.

### Host layer
How a CLI host uses this package.

Examples:
- OpenCode as host
- another compatible host agent
- later adapter-specific hosts

### Output layer
What the generated repo looks like.

The default output is intentionally OpenCode-oriented and may include:
- root `AGENTS.md`
- `opencode.jsonc`
- `.opencode/agents/`
- `.opencode/tools/`
- `.opencode/plugins/`
- `.opencode/commands/`
- `.opencode/skills/`
- `.opencode/state/`
- signposted docs and ticket surfaces

Do not collapse these layers together.

## First principles

1. Keep core package behavior host-agnostic unless a file is clearly adapter-specific.
2. Keep the default generated repo OpenCode-oriented.
3. Prefer one orchestrated full-cycle build over a base-pass-plus-manual-enrichment design.
4. Prefer deterministic workflow contracts over verbose prompting.
5. Preserve clear boundaries between skills.
6. Generate the smallest surface compatible with the selected profile; for the default full profile, keep heavier packs thin or lazy-activated until they are actually needed.
7. Never treat public skill discovery as permission to auto-install random skills.

## Default scaffold spine

These skills are the current backbone and should remain coherent as a chain:

- `scaffold-kickoff`
- `spec-pack-normalizer`
- `repo-scaffold-factory`
- `project-skill-bootstrap`
- `opencode-team-bootstrap`
- `agent-prompt-engineering`
- `ticket-pack-builder`
- `handoff-brief`

`scafforge-audit` and `scafforge-repair` remain separate post-generation lifecycle skills.
Optional extension skills may exist outside this default scaffold spine when they solve later host-side workflow needs without polluting the scaffold chain. These should stay clearly marked as optional.

## Canonical workflow contract

### Greenfield full-cycle scaffold

The default route should be:

1. `scaffold-kickoff` decides this is a greenfield build
2. `spec-pack-normalizer` produces a canonical brief
3. `repo-scaffold-factory` renders the base scaffold
4. `project-skill-bootstrap` completes the full greenfield local-skill pass
5. `opencode-team-bootstrap` designs the project-specific agent team
6. `agent-prompt-engineering` runs the required same-session prompt-hardening pass
7. `ticket-pack-builder` runs in bootstrap mode
8. `handoff-brief` refreshes the restart surface

A greenfield scaffold allows one batched blocking-decision round and then completes in one uninterrupted same-session pass. No second Scafforge generation pass is required before development begins.

## Product contract refinements

These refinements now govern implementation of the package contract:

- intake is **opportunistic first**: scan messy docs, notes, and fragmented inputs before normalizing them into a canonical brief
- meaningful ambiguity must be converted into a **batched decision packet** and asked, not silently assumed
- the default output remains **one full orchestration OpenCode scaffold**
- `scaffold-kickoff` remains the **single public entrypoint** for greenfield, retrofit, managed-repair/update, and diagnosis/review flows; downstream skills are routing targets, not user-facing starting points
- the greenfield path is **one-shot**: one batched blocking-decision round, one uninterrupted same-session generation pass, then direct handoff into development
- the generated repo must have a **structured truth hierarchy** with exact canonical owners for facts, queue state, transient workflow state, artifacts, provenance, and restart surfaces
- the initial backlog should be **implementation-ready where decisions are resolved**, while unresolved major choices become explicit blocked or decision tickets instead of fabricated detail
- `scafforge-audit` should own read-only diagnosis, review validation, and full diagnosis-pack generation on every audit run
- `scafforge-repair` should consume audit outputs, apply safe repairs, continue into required project-specific regeneration steps, and escalate intent-changing repairs
- package-level PR evidence intake should be folded into `scafforge-audit` instead of surviving as a separate primary skill
- standalone refinement routing should not remain as a package-level flow
- managed-surface process replacement must leave explicit version and verification state so the generated repo can tell when its workflow contract changed
- cross-host installability should come from **adapter packaging and bootstrap flows**, not by making the generated output multi-host

## Canonical generated-repo truth hierarchy

The generated repo should converge on these canonical roles:

- `docs/spec/CANONICAL-BRIEF.md` owns durable project facts, constraints, accepted decisions, and unresolved questions
- `tickets/manifest.json` owns machine queue state
- `tickets/BOARD.md` is the derived human queue board
- `.opencode/state/workflow-state.json` owns transient stage, approval, and process-version state
- `.opencode/state/artifacts/` plus manifest-backed registration own stage proof
- `.opencode/meta/bootstrap-provenance.json` owns provenance for scaffold, later synthesis, and repairs
- `START-HERE.md` is the derived restart surface

### Retrofit flow

Use the lighter path when a repo already exists and mainly needs an OpenCode operating layer:

1. `scaffold-kickoff` decides this is retrofit work
2. `spec-pack-normalizer` runs only if the input context is fragmented
3. `opencode-team-bootstrap` adds or repairs `.opencode/`
4. `ticket-pack-builder` runs if ticketing is missing or weak
5. `project-skill-bootstrap` creates or repairs local skills
6. `scafforge-audit` audits the resulting workflow
7. `handoff-brief` publishes restart state

### Managed repair / update flow

Use this path when a repo is already Scafforge-managed or otherwise OpenCode-oriented and mainly needs workflow-contract correction, managed-surface replacement, or process-version refresh:

1. `scaffold-kickoff` decides this is managed repair/update work
2. `scafforge-repair` runs for safe workflow repairs and managed-surface refresh
3. `project-skill-bootstrap` repairs local skills if needed
4. `opencode-team-bootstrap` follows up only if project-specific `.opencode/` drift remains
5. `agent-prompt-engineering` reruns when regenerated skills or agents changed prompt behavior
6. `ticket-pack-builder` repairs backlog state if needed
7. `handoff-brief` publishes restart state

### Diagnosis / review flow

Use this path when a repo needs read-only diagnosis, evidence validation, or the four-report diagnosis pack:

1. `scaffold-kickoff` decides this is diagnosis/review work
2. `scafforge-audit` validates findings and emits the diagnosis outputs
3. if package defects or prevention work are required, the user manually carries the diagnosis pack into the Scafforge dev repo
4. Scafforge package changes land there before any subject-repo repair run
5. `scafforge-repair` runs only if the audit still recommends workflow repair after those package changes exist
6. `ticket-pack-builder` follows up when remediation tickets are needed
7. `handoff-brief` publishes restart state when a closeout surface is needed

### Review / QA flow

Implementation, review, security, and QA procedure should be emitted as generated repo-local skills under `.opencode/skills/`, not carried as top-level Scafforge package skills.

## Skill boundary rules

### `scaffold-kickoff`
The conductor.

It should:
- classify the run type
- sequence downstream skills
- remain the single public entrypoint
- enforce required outputs
- avoid duplicating scaffold logic itself

It should not become a second scaffold engine.

### `spec-pack-normalizer`
Owns canonical brief creation.

It should not scaffold files or invent a ticket system.
It should also emit the ambiguity packet and backlog-readiness signal that downstream scaffold and ticket steps depend on.

### `repo-scaffold-factory`
Owns the base template and structural rendering.

It should remain the single source of truth for scaffold assets.

### `opencode-team-bootstrap`
Owns agent team design for the generated project.

In a greenfield flow, this runs after `repo-scaffold-factory` generates generic agent templates. It analyzes the project type and stack from the canonical brief, then customizes all agents to be project-specific. This includes adding domain-specific implementers, rewriting generic prompts, and reviewing tools/plugins for project-specific additions.
In a greenfield flow, this runs after the repo-local skill pack already exists, so agent skill allowlists can reference only generated local skills that are already present.

In a retrofit flow, this adds or repairs the `.opencode/` operating layer when the repo already exists.

### `ticket-pack-builder`
Owns ticket pack structure.

It must support:
- bootstrap mode during the first full-cycle scaffold
- refine mode later for backlog expansion or regeneration

It should not be treated as an alternate scaffold root.
It should convert unresolved major choices into explicit blocked or decision tickets rather than guessing at implementation detail.

### `project-skill-bootstrap`
Owns project-local skill generation.

It must support:
- one full greenfield pass that populates the baseline workflow pack and any required synthesized local skills
- targeted repair or regeneration later when an existing repo needs local-skill fixes

It should not blindly copy generic internet skills into the repo.
It should keep heavier orchestration packs thin or lazy-activated unless project evidence justifies more depth.
It owns output-repo review and QA local skills such as `review-audit-bridge` when those procedures belong inside the generated `.opencode/skills/` layer.

### `agent-prompt-engineering`
Owns prompt hardening, not overall flow control.

It remains a required step in the standard greenfield scaffold chain even when the resulting hardening pass is light.
In greenfield generation, it runs in the same session and finishes before ticket bootstrap and handoff.

### `scafforge-audit`
Owns workflow diagnosis, review validation, and diagnosis-pack generation.

It must remain read-only.
It should validate PR or review evidence against the actual repo before treating anything as a canonical finding.

### `scafforge-repair`
Owns workflow repair execution and post-audit follow-up.

It should consume audit outputs, apply safe managed-surface repairs, and leave explicit provenance and verification state.

### `handoff-brief`
Owns the restart surface and closeout summary, not planning.

## Public skill discovery policy

Scafforge may inspect public skill ecosystems and public documentation, but it must not confuse **discovery** with **deployment**.

### Allowed

- search public skill sources for patterns
- inspect project/framework documentation
- compare multiple candidate workflows
- synthesize a project-local skill based on evidence
- cite the evidence source in package notes when useful

### Not allowed by default

- auto-install random external skills directly into generated repos
- create local skills that merely paraphrase docs with no procedural value
- create multiple overlapping local skills with fuzzy boundaries
- adopt public skill assumptions without checking whether they match the project stack

## Local skill synthesis rules

When generating project-local skills:

1. Start with the smallest workflow pack that keeps the repo usable.
2. Prefer project evidence over generic best-practice prose.
3. Prefer procedure over reference dumping.
4. Synthesize from docs and external patterns only when the result is clearly repo-specific.
5. Keep the number of local skills low enough that a weaker model can navigate them.
6. Add or revise skills only when they reduce ambiguity, not just because more skills feel impressive.

## Editing priorities

When editing this package, prioritize in this order:

1. correctness of the generated scaffold
2. correctness of the one-cycle workflow contract
3. OpenCode compatibility of the generated output
4. clarity of skill boundaries
5. local skill synthesis quality
6. documentation polish

## What not to reintroduce

Do not add back:

- personal home-directory sync logic as core package behavior
- Codex self-improvement workflows as part of the core product
- unrelated generic utility skills in the core pack
- duplicate scaffold logic across multiple skills
- hidden workflow state spread across too many files
- unconditional public skill imports

## Maintenance checklist

When changing the package:

- verify the skill chain still makes sense end to end
- verify `scaffold-kickoff` still describes the real default workflow
- verify bootstrap-mode ticket generation happens in the full-cycle path
- verify project-skill synthesis rules are still conservative and evidence-based
- verify generated template paths still match current OpenCode conventions
- verify any runtime assumptions about tools/plugins are still current
- remove stale host-specific wording from core skills and docs

## Definition of done for package changes

A package change is not done until:

- the skill boundaries remain coherent
- the default flow still completes in one orchestrated cycle
- generated output remains OpenCode-oriented and structurally valid
- docs and templates agree with the actual package behavior
- obvious legacy wording or orphaned assumptions are removed


## JavaScript REPL (Node)
- Use `js_repl` for Node-backed JavaScript with top-level await in a persistent kernel.
- `js_repl` is a freeform/custom tool. Direct `js_repl` calls must send raw JavaScript tool input (optionally with first-line `// codex-js-repl: timeout_ms=15000`). Do not wrap code in JSON (for example `{"code":"..."}`), quotes, or markdown code fences.
- Helpers: `codex.cwd`, `codex.homeDir`, `codex.tmpDir`, `codex.tool(name, args?)`, and `codex.emitImage(imageLike)`.
- `codex.tool` executes a normal tool call and resolves to the raw tool output object. Use it for shell and non-shell tools alike. Nested tool outputs stay inside JavaScript unless you emit them explicitly.
- `codex.emitImage(...)` adds one image to the outer `js_repl` function output each time you call it, so you can call it multiple times to emit multiple images. It accepts a data URL, a single `input_image` item, an object like `{ bytes, mimeType }`, or a raw tool response object with exactly one image and no text. It rejects mixed text-and-image content.
- `codex.tool(...)` and `codex.emitImage(...)` keep stable helper identities across cells. Saved references and persisted objects can reuse them in later cells, but async callbacks that fire after a cell finishes still fail because no exec is active.
- Request full-resolution image processing with `detail: "original"` only when the `view_image` tool schema includes a `detail` argument. The same availability applies to `codex.emitImage(...)`: if `view_image.detail` is present, you may also pass `detail: "original"` there. Use this when high-fidelity image perception or precise localization is needed, especially for CUA agents.
- Example of sharing an in-memory Playwright screenshot: `await codex.emitImage({ bytes: await page.screenshot({ type: "jpeg", quality: 85 }), mimeType: "image/jpeg", detail: "original" })`.
- Example of sharing a local image tool result: `await codex.emitImage(codex.tool("view_image", { path: "/absolute/path", detail: "original" }))`.
- When encoding an image to send with `codex.emitImage(...)` or `view_image`, prefer JPEG at about 85 quality when lossy compression is acceptable; use PNG when transparency or lossless detail matters. Smaller uploads are faster and less likely to hit size limits.
- Top-level bindings persist across cells. If a cell throws, prior bindings remain available and bindings that finished initializing before the throw often remain usable in later cells. For code you plan to reuse across cells, prefer declaring or assigning it in direct top-level statements before operations that might throw. If you hit `SyntaxError: Identifier 'x' has already been declared`, first reuse the existing binding, reassign a previously declared `let`, or pick a new descriptive name. Use `{ ... }` only for a short temporary block when you specifically need local scratch names; do not wrap an entire cell in block scope if you want those names reusable later. Reset the kernel with `js_repl_reset` only when you need a clean state.
- Top-level static import declarations (for example `import x from "./file.js"`) are currently unsupported in `js_repl`; use dynamic imports with `await import("pkg")`, `await import("./file.js")`, or `await import("/abs/path/file.mjs")` instead. Imported local files must be ESM `.js`/`.mjs` files and run in the same REPL VM context. Bare package imports always resolve from REPL-global search roots (`CODEX_JS_REPL_NODE_MODULE_DIRS`, then cwd), not relative to the imported file location. Local files may statically import only other local relative/absolute/`file://` `.js`/`.mjs` files; package and builtin imports from local files must stay dynamic. `import.meta.resolve()` returns importable strings such as `file://...`, bare package names, and `node:...` specifiers. Local file modules reload between execs, while top-level bindings persist until `js_repl_reset`.
- Avoid direct access to `process.stdout` / `process.stderr` / `process.stdin`; it can corrupt the JSON line protocol. Use `console.log`, `codex.tool(...)`, and `codex.emitImage(...)`.

./secondreview.md:28:### C2. Lease delegation conflict (team-leader and specialist both claim)
./secondreview.md:41:**Nuance:** The `ensureWriteLease` function in stage-gate-enforcer checks `hasWriteLeaseForTicket` WITHOUT an `ownerAgent` parameter, meaning ANY lease for the ticket satisfies it. So if the team-leader holds the lease, the specialist's artifact_write calls SHOULD pass the plugin check. BUT the specialist's own prompt tells it to claim first, which fails. The specialist then concludes it can't write and reports a blocker.
./secondreview.md:50:### C3. Planning-stage bootstrap circular dependency
./secondreview.md:52:**Where:** stage-gate-enforcer.ts lines 97-104, 117-127; `ticket_claim` lines 47-49
./secondreview.md:59:5. Agent tries manual fix via bash → `uv sync --locked --extra dev` → blocked by stage-gate-enforcer: "active ticket needs an approved plan before running implementation-oriented shell commands"
./secondreview.md:72:### C4. Backlog-verification write path blocked on done tickets
./secondreview.md:74:**Where:** stage-gate-enforcer.ts lines 225-238, ticket_claim.ts lines 35-37
./secondreview.md:77:1. After a process version change, `pending_process_verification: true` and 51 tickets need reverification
./secondreview.md:79:3. `artifact_write` triggers stage-gate-enforcer → `ensureTargetTicketWriteLease` → needs lease on ticket
./secondreview.md:83:**Workaround exists but is partial:** `ticket_reverify` tool writes its own artifact internally and skips the lease check in stage-gate-enforcer. BUT it requires `evidence_artifact_path` pointing to an existing registered artifact — which the backlog verifier was supposed to CREATE.
./secondreview.md:93:### C5. handoff_publish blocked by ANY pending reverification
./secondreview.md:95:**Where:** stage-gate-enforcer.ts lines 315-318
./secondreview.md:97:**What happens:** `handoff_publish` checks ALL tickets for `needs_reverification`. If even ONE done ticket needs it, handoff is blocked. With 51 suspect tickets in GPTTalker, handoff is permanently impossible until all 51 are individually reverified. In sequential mode, that's 51 sequential claim-verify-release cycles.
./secondreview.md:108:### C6. No lease expiry/timeout for crash recovery
./secondreview.md:112:**What happens:** If a session crashes mid-lease, the lease persists in workflow-state.json forever. The next session inherits a stale lease. If the new session's agent name matches, it can release. But there's no TTL, no stale-detection, no auto-cleanup.
./secondreview.md:189:**Where:** stage-gate-enforcer.ts lines 277-284; agent prompts for reviewer and QA
./secondreview.md:234:### M1. Sequential parallel_mode prevents practical verification
./secondreview.md:238:**What:** Only ONE lease active at a time. Backlog verification of 51 tickets requires 51 sequential claim-verify-release cycles. Combined with C4 (done tickets can't be claimed), verification is structurally impossible.
./secondreview.md:251:**What this means:** The audit-to-repair cycle is not converging. Each repair fixes surface symptoms but doesn't address the structural deadlocks (C1-C6). The next session hits the same deadlocks and triggers another diagnosis.
./secondreview.md:258:### M3. Template bootstrap status "pending" not in BootstrapStatus type
./secondreview.md:272:### M4. Artifact registry version mismatch
./secondreview.md:301:**Where:** All agent prompts, stage-gate-enforcer.ts
./secondreview.md:303:**What:** When agents hit deadlocks (C1-C5), they have no legitimate way to escalate. No "report blocker to human" command, no "request exception" mechanism. Agents either stay permanently stuck or attempt creative workarounds that break downstream gates.

Restore string-backed status handling and deterministic missing-parameter responses so the hub tools match the contract-tested surface again.\n\n**Acceptance Criteria**:\n1. `UV_CACHE_DIR=/tmp/uv-cache uv run pytest tests/hub/test_contracts.py -q --tb=no -k 'list_nodes_returns_structured_response or inspect_repo_tree_requires_node_and_repo or read_repo_file_requires_parameters'` exits 0.\n2. `list_nodes_handler()` accepts string-backed node status and health values without attribute errors and keeps the structured response shape.\n3. `inspect_repo_tree_handler()` and `read_repo_file_handler()` return deterministic contract-aligned not-found or validation errors for missing identifiers instead of dependency-availability errors.\n4. The fix preserves existing node ownership and repo-boundary checks.\n\n**Known Facts**:\n- EXEC-004 fixed `PathNormalizer.normalize()` for repo-relative paths\n- EXEC-005 fixed `write_markdown_handler` parameter names and `format_tool_response` nesting\n- The failing tests are in `tests/hub/test_contracts.py` — specifically `list_nodes_returns_structured_response`, `inspect_repo_tree_requires_node_and_repo`, `read_repo_file_requires_parameters`\n- `src/hub/tools/discovery.py` contains `list_nodes_handler` and `list_repos_handler`\n- `src/hub/tools/inspection.py` contains `inspect_repo_tree_handler` and `read_repo_file_handler`\n- `pending_process_verification: true` affects 23 done tickets but this ticket (EXEC-007) is not blocked by reverification — it can proceed\n\n**Constraints**:\n- Only modify `src/hub/tools/discovery.py`, `src/hub/tools/inspection.py`, `src/hub/tools/__init__.py`\n- Do not touch node-agent code, shared schemas, or policy engine\n- Do not widen trust boundaries or bypass existing node-ownership / repo-boundary checks\n- Preserve all existing fail-closed behaviors\n- Use `UV_CACHE_DIR=/tmp/uv-cache uv run` for all validation commands\n\n**Expected Output**:\n- Write a planning artifact at `.opencode/state/plans/exec-007-planning-plan.md` using `artifact_write` with `kind: planning` and `stage: planning`\n- Register it with `artifact_register` for EXEC-007\n- The plan must include: root-cause analysis of each failing contract case, the specific code changes needed, file-by-file change清单, and a validation plan showing how to confirm each acceptance criterion\n\n**Artifact stage**: planning  \n**Artifact kind**: planning  \n**Canonical path**: `.opencode/state/plans/exec-007-planning-plan.md`",

Command: /bin/bash -lc "nl -ba /home/pc/projects/Scafforge/secondreview.md | sed -n '1,340p'"
Chunk ID: 7d5c41

Output:
     1	###### TASK
     2	
     3	The following is a second review carried out by another AI. The majority of the findings need investigating and confirming. I have marked some of them as not valid with comments formatted as {USER COMMENT: this is a user comment}.
     4	
     5	You should investigate each finding in depth. If it is valid, add it to the existing plan. If not, you must cite specific evidence for why it is not valid (e.g. not a valid finding, or was already addressed - this must be conclusively proven by citing evidence).
     6	
     7	All valid findings to be added to scafforgeplan.md
     8	
     9	
    10	
    11	##### Findings
    12	
    13	### C1. `uv sync --locked` strips dev dependencies (ROOT CAUSE of most failures)    
    14	                                                                                                    
    15	**Where:** `skills/repo-scaffold-factory/assets/project-template/.opencode/tools/environment_bootstrap.ts` lines 104-135
    16	**What happens:** `environment_bootstrap` runs `uv sync --locked` which removes packages not in the default dependency group. pytest, pytest-asyncio, ruff (7 packages) get UNINSTALLED. The tool conditionally adds `--extra dev` only when `hasPyprojectDevExtra()` returns true, but this detection checks for `[project.optional-dependencies]` with a `dev = [...]` key. If the project uses `[tool.uv.dev-dependencies]` (the uv-native way) or a different group name, the detection fails.
    17	**Evidence:** continuallylocked.md shows "uv sync --locked" uninstalling 7 packages; 2603log.md shows 12+ bootstrap failures; sessionlog0458.md shows the same pattern.
    18	
    19	**Impact:** After stripping, bootstrap reports pytest missing → bootstrap status = failed → all non-wave-0 lease claims blocked → lifecycle deadlocked.
    20	
    21	**Fix needed:** Always pass `--extra dev` when a dev group exists (check both `[project.optional-dependencies].dev` AND `[tool.uv.dev-dependencies]`). Add a `--all-extras` fallback. Have the bootstrap tool verify critical tools are still present AFTER sync and report the regression explicitly.
    22	
    23	**scafforgeplan.md coverage:** Not mentioned at all.
    24	
    25	
    26	
    27	
    28	### C2. Lease delegation conflict (team-leader and specialist both claim)
    29	
    30	**Where:** `_workflow.ts` line 637, team-leader prompt, lane-executor prompt, implementer prompt
    31	
    32	**What happens:**
    33	1. Team-leader prompt says: "grant a write lease with `ticket_claim` before any specialist writes"
    34	2. Lane-executor prompt says: "claim the assigned ticket with `ticket_claim` before any write-capable work"
    35	3. Both try to claim → `claimLaneLease` line 637: "Ticket X already has an active lease owned by {team-leader}. Release it before changing owners."
    36	4. The team-leader doesn't release before delegating, and the specialist can't write without claiming.
    37	
    38	**Evidence:** sessionlog0458.md shows "write lease conflicts" during delegation. gpttalkerlog1.md shows planner blocked by existing team-leader lease.
    39	
    40	
    41	**Nuance:** The `ensureWriteLease` function in stage-gate-enforcer checks `hasWriteLeaseForTicket` WITHOUT an `ownerAgent` parameter, meaning ANY lease for the ticket satisfies it. So if the team-leader holds the lease, the specialist's artifact_write calls SHOULD pass the plugin check. BUT the specialist's own prompt tells it to claim first, which fails. The specialist then concludes it can't write and reports a blocker.
    42	                                                                                            **Fix needed:** Either (a) team-leader releases before delegating and specialist claims, (b) specialists skip claiming and rely on team-leader's lease (update all specialist prompts), or (c) implement lease transfer/delegation in `ticket_claim`. Option (b) is simplest and most robust.
    43	
    44	**scafforgeplan.md coverage:** Not mentioned. scafforgeplan.md discusses coordinator liveness but not the delegation handshake.
    45	
    46	
    47	
    48	
    49	
    50	### C3. Planning-stage bootstrap circular dependency
    51	
    52	**Where:** stage-gate-enforcer.ts lines 97-104, 117-127; `ticket_claim` lines 47-49
    53	
    54	**What happens for a non-wave-0 ticket when bootstrap is failed:**
    55	1. Agent needs to write a planning artifact → needs write lease → `ticket_claim` with `write_lock: true`
    56	2. `ticket_claim` checks: `allowsPreBootstrapWriteClaim` → false (not wave 0) → calls `requireBootstrapReady` → throws "Bootstrap failed"
    57	3. Agent tries to fix bootstrap → runs `environment_bootstrap` (tool call, not blocked by bash guard)
    58	4. But `environment_bootstrap` runs `uv sync --locked` which strips dev deps (C1) → bootstrap fails again
    59	5. Agent tries manual fix via bash → `uv sync --locked --extra dev` → blocked by stage-gate-enforcer: "active ticket needs an approved plan before running implementation-oriented shell commands"
    60	6. Agent can't get approved plan because it can't write the planning artifact (step 1)
    61	7. **Deadlock: no valid transition exists**
    62	
    63	**Evidence:** continuallylocked.md: "The shell command is being blocked because EXEC-007 is in planning stage with no approved plan"; sessionlog0458.md: 12+ bootstrap failure loops.
    64	**Fix needed:** Make `environment_bootstrap` a special privileged tool that always works regardless of plan/lease state. Add `uv`, `pytest`, `python` to SAFE_BASH when the active purpose is bootstrap recovery. Or: allow read-only lease claims for planning even when bootstrap is failed (planning doesn't need runtime).
    65	
    66	**scafforgeplan.md coverage:** Not mentioned at all.
    67	
    68	
    69	
    70	
    71	
    72	### C4. Backlog-verification write path blocked on done tickets
    73	
    74	**Where:** stage-gate-enforcer.ts lines 225-238, ticket_claim.ts lines 35-37
    75	
    76	**What happens:**
    77	1. After a process version change, `pending_process_verification: true` and 51 tickets need reverification
    78	2. Backlog-verifier agent is told to "write and register a `review` artifact with kind `backlog-verification`"
    79	3. `artifact_write` triggers stage-gate-enforcer → `ensureTargetTicketWriteLease` → needs lease on ticket
    80	4. `ticket_claim` refuses: "Ticket X cannot be claimed because it is already closed"
    81	5. Backlog verifier cannot write verification artifacts for done tickets
    82	
    83	**Workaround exists but is partial:** `ticket_reverify` tool writes its own artifact internally and skips the lease check in stage-gate-enforcer. BUT it requires `evidence_artifact_path` pointing to an existing registered artifact — which the backlog verifier was supposed to CREATE.
    84	
    85	**Fix needed:** Either (a) add a `system` lease type that bypasses the closed-ticket restriction for verification work, (b) make `ticket_reverify` self-contained (accepts verification text, not just a reference to a pre-existing artifact), or (c) exempt `artifact_write` from lease checks when the artifact kind is `backlog-verification` and the ticket is done.
    86	
    87	**scafforgeplan.md coverage:** Not mentioned. scafforgeplan.md discusses post-repair verification gaps but not the mechanical impossibility.
    88	
    89	
    90	
    91	
    92	
    93	### C5. handoff_publish blocked by ANY pending reverification
    94	
    95	**Where:** stage-gate-enforcer.ts lines 315-318
    96	
    97	**What happens:** `handoff_publish` checks ALL tickets for `needs_reverification`. If even ONE done ticket needs it, handoff is blocked. With 51 suspect tickets in GPTTalker, handoff is permanently impossible until all 51 are individually reverified. In sequential mode, that's 51 sequential claim-verify-release cycles.
    98	
    99	
   100	**Fix needed:** Allow handoff when the active ticket's verification is clean, even if historical verification is incomplete. Or: batch-verify tickets rather than one at a time.
   101	
   102	**scafforgeplan.md coverage:** Not mentioned.
   103	
   104	
   105	
   106	
   107	
   108	### C6. No lease expiry/timeout for crash recovery
   109	
   110	**Where:** `_workflow.ts` lines 623-644; GPTTalker workflow-state.json
   111	
   112	**What happens:** If a session crashes mid-lease, the lease persists in workflow-state.json forever. The next session inherits a stale lease. If the new session's agent name matches, it can release. But there's no TTL, no stale-detection, no auto-cleanup.
   113	**Evidence:** GPTTalker workflow-state.json contains a lease from `2026-03-26T04:25:15.647Z` — hours stale.
   114	
   115	**Fix needed:** Add `expires_at` or `ttl_minutes` to `LaneLease`. Have `loadWorkflowState` prune expired leases on load. Add a `/release-stale-leases` command for human recovery. {USER NOTE: Can we make this a tool instead for team leader? In general, /commands are NOT preferred and the ability should go to the AI agent, with strict guard/usage restrictions. The purpose of Scafforge is an autonomous development cycle minimizing human in the loop}
   116	
   117	**scafforgeplan.md coverage:** Not mentioned.
   118	
   119	---  
   120	
   121	
   122	                                                                                       
   123	## HIGH — Causes Repeated Failures
   124	
   125	### H1. opencode.jsonc bash permissions conflict with agent permissions
   126	
   127	**Where:** template `opencode.jsonc` (project-level), all agent `.md` files (agent-level)
   128	
   129	**What:** Project-level bash: `"*": "deny"`, only `pwd|ls|find|rg|sed|cat|head|tail|git status|git diff|git log` allowed. Agent-level (e.g., lane-executor): `"python *": allow`, `"pytest *": allow`, `"uv *": allow`, `"ruff *": allow`.
   130	
   131	**Problem:** If OpenCode intersects (most restrictive wins), agent-level bash allowlists are meaningless — python, pytest, uv, ruff are all blocked at project level. The actual behavior depends on how OpenCode resolves the conflict, which is undocumented in Scafforge.
   132	
   133	**Evidence:** Session logs show bash commands blocked that SHOULD be allowed per agent permissions. GPTTalker's opencode.jsonc has the exact same restrictive project-level permissions.
   134	
   135	**Fix needed:** Either (a) add `python`, `pytest`, `uv`, `ruff`, `npm`, `node` to project-level bash allowlist, or (b) document and test that agent-level permissions override project-level for bash, or (c) remove project-level bash restrictions and rely on agent-level controls.
   136	
   137	**scafforgeplan.md coverage:** Not mentioned.
   138	
   139	
   140	
   141	
   142	
   143	### H2. smoke_test tool: binary full-suite pass/fail, no scoped evaluation
   144	
   145	**Where:** template `smoke_test.ts` lines 127-150, 377-390
   146	
   147	**What:** The smoke test runs the ENTIRE pytest suite. If any test fails — including pre-existing failures from other tickets — the artifact records FAIL. An agent can fix all bugs in its ticket perfectly, but unrelated failures from other tickets block closeout.
   148	
   149	**Evidence:** 2603log.md, gpttalkerlog1.md, sessionlog0458.md all show smoke test FAIL from tests outside the active ticket's scope. Agents then try to force closeout or rationalize failures — both bad outcomes.
   150	
   151	**Fix needed:** Add a `--scope` or `--test-paths` parameter to smoke_test that lets it run only the tests relevant to the active ticket. Keep full-suite as default but allow scoped runs.
   152	
   153	**scafforgeplan.md coverage:** Not mentioned.
   154	
   155	
   156	
   157	
   158	
   159	### H3. smoke_test doesn't pass `--extra dev` to uv
   160	
   161	**Where:** template `smoke_test.ts` lines 128-133
   162	
   163	**What:** Uses `["uv", "run", "python"]` as the runner. Doesn't pass `--extra dev`. If dev dependencies weren't synced (C1), pytest isn't available during smoke test.
   164	
   165	**Fix needed:** Use `["uv", "run", "--extra", "dev", "python"]` when a dev dependency group is detected.
   166	
   167	**scafforgeplan.md coverage:** Not mentioned.
   168	
   169	
   170	
   171	
   172	
   173	### H4. `_workflow_*` tools fail with "def.execute is not a function"
   174	
   175	**Where:** Generated `.opencode/tools/_workflow.ts` — no `export default tool(...)`
   176	
   177	**What:** Session logs show `_workflow_loadWorkflowState` and `_workflow_readArtifactContent` failing with `def.execute is not a function`. OpenCode exposes the helpers as tools but they have no `execute` method.
   178	
   179	**Evidence:** gptlog.md, gptlog2.md, sessionlog0458.md all show this error pattern.
   180	
   181	**Connection to scafforgeplan.md Stream 1:** This IS the same issue as helper leakage. scafforgeplan.md identifies it correctly but doesn't note the specific runtime error pattern agents encounter.
   182	
   183	
   184	
   185	
   186	
   187	### H5. QA/review artifacts accepted without runtime evidence
   188	
   189	**Where:** stage-gate-enforcer.ts lines 277-284; agent prompts for reviewer and QA
   190	
   191	**What:** Stage-gate checks that an implementation/review artifact EXISTS before allowing transition to the next stage. But it doesn't validate the CONTENT. When bash restrictions prevent test execution, agents write "APPROVED" artifacts based on static code reading only. Quote from 2603log: "Due to bash policy restrictions, the runtime validation commands could not be executed. Static analysis confirms correctness." — followed by smoke test showing regressions.
   192	
   193	**Fix needed:** Extend `validateImplementationArtifactEvidence` and `validateQaArtifactEvidence` to check for markers of actual runtime execution (command output, test counts, etc.). Or: require that review/QA artifacts reference specific test output files.
   194	
   195	**scafforgeplan.md coverage:** Partially covered under "coordinator liveness" but not as a validation gap.
   196	
   197	
   198	### H6. Audit/repair reference files have diverged
   199	
   200	**Where:**
   201	- `scafforge-audit/references/repair-playbook.md` has WFLOW015; `scafforge-repair/references/repair-playbook.md` does NOT
   202

   215	### H7. No git commit in allowed bash commands
   216	
   217	**Where:** template `opencode.jsonc`, all agent bash permissions
   218	
   219	**What:** No agent and no project-level permission allows `git commit`, `git add`, or `git push`. All agent work produces uncommitted local changes. This means: no incremental save points, session crashes lose all work, no meaningful git log of progress, no way to create PRs.
   220	
   221	**Fix needed:** Add `git add`, `git commit` to team-leader and lane-executor bash permissions. Keep `git push`, `git reset`, `git clean` denied.

   230	## MEDIUM — Design Weaknesses
   231

   234	### M1. Sequential parallel_mode prevents practical verification
   235	
   236	**Where:** workflow-state.json template `"parallel_mode": "sequential"`, claimLaneLease line 625
   237	
   238	**What:** Only ONE lease active at a time. Backlog verification of 51 tickets requires 51 sequential claim-verify-release cycles. Combined with C4 (done tickets can't be claimed), verification is structurally impossible.

   248	
   249	**Evidence:** 25 diagnosis directories in GPTTalker. Multiple same-day re-runs (e.g., 5 runs on 2026-03-25 evening). Latest diagnosis still reports unresolved issues.
   250	
   251	**What this means:** The audit-to-repair cycle is not converging. Each repair fixes surface symptoms but doesn't address the structural deadlocks (C1-C6). The next session hits the same deadlocks and triggers another diagnosis.

   259	
   260	**Where:** template workflow-state.json line 19: `"status": "pending"`, _workflow.ts type: `"missing" | "ready" | "stale" | "failed"`
   261	
   262	**What:** The template ships a value that doesn't exist in its own type system. `loadWorkflowState` silently normalizes to "missing". Misleading but not breaking.
   263	
   264	**Fix needed:** Change template to `"status": "missing"`.

Correct this assertion if I am wrong.}

That's 10+ files before any work begins. For a weaker model with 32K context, this could consume 40-60% of available context.

   300	
   301	**Where:** All agent prompts, stage-gate-enforcer.ts
   302	
   303	**What:** When agents hit deadlocks (C1-C5), they have no legitimate way to escalate. No "report blocker to human" command, no "request exception" mechanism. Agents either stay permanently stuck or attempt creative workarounds that break downstream gates.
   304	
   305	**Evidence:** 4/7 session logs show agents attempting bypass strategies. gpttalkerlog1.md: "I literally cannot make ANY write operations in the repo at this point."
   306	
   307	**Fix needed:** Add a `/report-blocker` command that logs the blocker, suspends the active ticket, and asks the human operator for intervention. Make this available to all agents.

    19	    allowed_paths: tool.schema.array(tool.schema.string()).describe("Repo-relative path prefixes this lease may edit.").optional(),
    20	    write_lock: tool.schema.boolean().describe("Whether this lease authorizes file edits. Defaults to true.").optional(),
    21	  },
    22	  async execute(args) {
    23	    const manifest = await loadManifest()
    24	    const workflow = await loadWorkflowState()
    25	    const ticket = getTicket(manifest, args.ticket_id)
    26	    const ownerAgent = args.owner_agent.trim()
    27	    const writeLock = args.write_lock !== false
    28	
    29	    if (!ownerAgent) {
    30	      throw new Error("owner_agent must not be empty.")
    31	    }
    32	    if (ticket.decision_blockers.length > 0 && ticket.status === "blocked") {
    33	      throw new Error(`Ticket ${ticket.id} is blocked and cannot be claimed until its decision blockers are resolved.`)
    34	    }
    35	    if (ticket.status === "done" || ticket.resolution_state === "superseded") {
    36	      throw new Error(`Ticket ${ticket.id} cannot be claimed because it is already closed.`)
    37	    }
    38	    for (const dependency of ticket.depends_on) {
    39	      const dependencyTicket = getTicket(manifest, dependency)
    40	      if (dependencyTicket.status !== "done") {
    41	        throw new Error(`Ticket ${ticket.id} cannot be claimed before dependency ${dependencyTicket.id} is done.`)
    42	      }
    43	      if (dependencyTicket.verification_state === "invalidated" || getTicketWorkflowState(workflow, dependencyTicket.id).needs_reverification) {
    44	        throw new Error(`Ticket ${ticket.id} cannot be claimed because dependency ${dependencyTicket.id} is no longer trusted.`)
    45	      }
    46	    }
    47	    if (writeLock && !allowsPreBootstrapWriteClaim(workflow, ticket)) {
    48	      await requireBootstrapReady(workflow, rootPath())
    49	    }
    50	
    51	    const lease = claimLaneLease(workflow, ticket, ownerAgent, args.allowed_paths || [], writeLock)
    52	    await saveWorkflowState(workflow)
    53

    18	  evidenceTicketId: string
    19	  evidenceArtifactPath: string
    20	  reason: string
    21	}): string {
    22	  return `# Ticket Reverification
    23	
    24	## Source Ticket
    25	
    26	- ${args.sourceTicketId}
    27	
    28	## Evidence
    29

    34

    38

    40	`
    41	}
    42	
    43	export default tool({
    44	  description: "Restore trust for a completed historical ticket after follow-up remediation or backlog reverification completes. This is the legal mutation path for closed done tickets that still need process verification.",
    45	  args: {

    47	    evidence_artifact_path: tool.schema.string().describe("Current artifact path proving the reverification outcome."),

    53	    const workflow = await loadWorkflowState()

    55	    const evidenceTicket = getTicket(manifest, args.evidence_ticket_id || sourceTicket.id)
    56	    const evidencePath = normalizeRepoPath(args.evidence_artifact_path)
    57	    const reason = typeof args.reason === "string" && args.reason.trim()
    58	      ? args.reason.trim()

    60

    70	    }
    71	    if (evidenceTicket.id !== sourceTicket.id && evidenceTicket.status !== "done") {
    72	      throw new Error(`Evidence ticket ${evidenceTicket.id} must be done before it can restore trust for ${sourceTicket.id}.`)
    73	    }
    74
